description: Add src/gr/gauss_codazzi.jl with: (1) gauss_equation(reg, hyp) returning RewriteRule: projected ambient Riemann → intrinsic Riemann + extrinsic curvature terms: R^(γ)_{ijkl} = P^a_i P^b_j P^c_k P^d_l R_{abcd} + K_{ik}K_{jl} - K_{il}K_{jk}; (2) codazzi_equation(reg, hyp) returning ∇_i K_{jk} - ∇_j K_{ik} = P^a_i P^b_j P^c_k n^d R_{abcd}; (3) Register as rules via register_rule!. These relate ambient and intrinsic curvature on embedded hypersurfaces. ~200 lines.
title: Implement Gauss-Codazzi relations as rewrite rules

description: Implement SpinIndex type for dotted (primed) and undotted (unprimed) spinor indices with Up/Down positions. Add to src/spinors/types.jl or src/types.jl. SpinIndex needs: name::Symbol, position::IndexPosition (Up/Down), conjugated::Bool (primed vs unprimed), vbundle::Symbol (default :SpinBundle). Convenience constructors: spin_up(:A), spin_down(:A), spin_up_dot(:Ap), spin_down_dot(:Ap). ~80 LOC. Reference: Penrose & Rindler Vol 1 (1984), Sec 2.5. Test: construct indices, verify position/conjugation flags, test equality/hashing.
title: Spinors: Implement SpinIndex type (dotted/undotted)

description:
DESIGN DECISION: How spin coefficients (12 complex NP scalars) relate to Ricci rotation coefficients.

Background:
  Ricci rotation coefficients: gamma_{abc} = e^mu_a e^nu_b nabla_mu e_{c nu}
  These are the connection in the frame basis (3-index object, dim^3 components).
  For metric-compatible torsion-free connection: gamma_{abc} = (1/2)(c_{abc} + c_{cab} - c_{bca})
  where c^a_{bc} = [e_b, e_c]^a are anholonomy coefficients.

Spin coefficients (Newman-Penrose):
  The 12 complex spin coefficients (kappa, sigma, lambda, nu, rho, mu, tau, pi, epsilon, gamma_NP, alpha, beta) are specific projections of gamma_{abc} onto the null tetrad:
    kappa = gamma_{311},  sigma = gamma_{313},  lambda = gamma_{244},  nu = gamma_{242}
    rho = gamma_{314},    mu = gamma_{243},      tau = gamma_{312},     pi = gamma_{241}
    epsilon = (1/2)(gamma_{211} + gamma_{341})
    gamma_NP = (1/2)(gamma_{212} + gamma_{342})
    alpha = (1/2)(gamma_{214} + gamma_{344})
    beta = (1/2)(gamma_{213} + gamma_{343})
  (using NP convention: 1=l, 2=n, 3=m, 4=mbar)

Design:
1. ricci_rotation_coefficients(tetrad) -> Array{T,3} of dim x dim x dim
   Computed from anholonomy_coefficients + permutation formula (no Christoffel needed).
2. spin_coefficients(tetrad) -> NamedTuple with all 12 NP coefficients
   Extracts from Ricci rotation coefficients using the NP index map above.
   Only valid for :null tetrads (error for :orthonormal).
3. Store as NamedTuple{(:kappa, :sigma, :lambda, :nu, :rho, :mu_NP, :tau, :pi_NP, :epsilon, :gamma_NP, :alpha, :beta)} to avoid clash with Julia's pi and gamma.

Existing code in src/xideal/weyl_scalars.jl already uses null tetrads for Weyl scalar computation. The spin coefficients extend this to the full NP formalism.

References: Newman & Penrose, J. Math. Phys. 3, 566 (1962); Chandrasekhar Ch 1 Sec 7; Stephani et al. Ch 7.
title: Design: Spin coefficients as special Ricci rotation coefficients

description: Implement the 12 complex NP spin coefficients as projections of the covariant derivative of the null tetrad onto the tetrad basis. The 12 coefficients are: kappa = m^a l^b nabla_b l_a, sigma = m^a m^b nabla_b l_a, lambda = mbar^a mbar^b nabla_b n_a, nu = mbar^a n^b nabla_b n_a, rho = m^a mbar^b nabla_b l_a, mu = mbar^a m^b nabla_b n_a, tau = m^a n^b nabla_b l_a, pi = mbar^a l^b nabla_b n_a, epsilon = (1/2)(n^a l^b nabla_b l_a - mbar^a l^b nabla_b m_a), gamma = (1/2)(n^a n^b nabla_b l_a - mbar^a n^b nabla_b m_a), alpha = (1/2)(n^a mbar^b nabla_b l_a - mbar^a mbar^b nabla_b m_a), beta = (1/2)(n^a m^b nabla_b l_a - mbar^a m^b nabla_b m_a). Register as named scalars. Function: compute_spin_coefficients(tetrad; registry). ~100 LOC in src/spinors/np.jl. Reference: NP (1962) Eq 4.2. Test: string-match each definition against NP paper.
title: NP: Implement 12 spin coefficients

description: Register the scalar curvature spinor Lambda = R/24 where R is the Ricci scalar. This is a scalar (no spinor indices) that appears in the full spinor decomposition of the Riemann tensor. Register with TensorRegistry: name=:Lambda_spin, no indices (scalar). Add simplification rule: Lambda_spin = (1/24) * RicScalar. ~40 LOC in src/spinors/curvature_spinors.jl. Reference: Penrose & Rindler Vol 1 (1984), Eq 4.6.26. Test: (1) Lambda = R/24 rule applies correctly, (2) vacuum spacetime: Lambda = 0 when R = 0.
title: Spinors: Register scalar curvature spinor Lambda

close_reason: 6 cubic curvature invariants added to catalog: R^3, R*Ric^2, Ric^3, R*Riem^2, Ric*Riem^2, Riem^3
description: Extend src/gr/invariants.jl with the 6 independent cubic invariants (already computed in benchmarks/bench_12_6deriv_dS.jl): I₁=R³, I₂=R·Ric², I₃=Ric³ (trace), I₄=R·Riem², I₅=Ric·Riem² (contraction), I₆=Riem³ (cycle). For each: (1) Add InvariantEntry with expression builder using registered curvature tensors; (2) Add dimension constraints (some vanish in 3D); (3) Add Bueno-Cano coefficient metadata from BC_CUBIC_COEFFICIENTS. Port the build_I1..build_I6 functions from bench_12 into the invariant catalog. ~200 lines.
title: Add order-3 cubic curvature invariants to catalog

description: Implement the 4 NP commutator relations expressing commutators of directional derivatives in terms of spin coefficients: [D,Delta]f = (gamma+gamma_bar)Df + (epsilon+epsilon_bar)Delta_f - (tau_bar+pi)delta_f - (tau+pi_bar)deltabar_f, and similarly for [D,delta], [delta,deltabar], [Delta,delta]. These are needed to manipulate NP equations and verify integrability. Function: np_commutators(; registry) -> Vector of commutator rules. ~60 LOC in src/spinors/np_equations.jl. Reference: NP (1962) Eq 4.1 (4 commutator equations). Test: STRING-MATCH each commutator against NP (1962) Eq 4.1a-4.1d.
title: NP: Implement NP commutator relations

description: Implement the Newman-Penrose null tetrad: l^a (real null), n^a (real null), m^a (complex null), mbar^a = conjugate(m^a). Normalization: l_a n^a = -1, m_a mbar^a = 1, all other inner products zero. Completeness: g^{ab} = -l^a n^b - n^a l^b + m^a mbar^b + mbar^a m^b. Register all four vectors with TensorRegistry. Function: define_null_tetrad!(reg; manifold, metric). ~80 LOC in src/spinors/np.jl. Reference: Newman & Penrose, J. Math. Phys. 3, 566 (1962), Eq 2.1. Test: (1) l_a n^a = -1 after contraction, (2) m_a mbar^a = 1, (3) l_a l^a = 0 (null), (4) completeness relation g^{ab} = -l^a n^b - n^a l^b + m^a mbar^b + mbar^a m^b.
title: NP: Implement null tetrad (l, n, m, mbar)

description:
## Summary

Provide a one-liner macro that sets up the complete spinor infrastructure on a
manifold, analogous to @manifold + define_metric! for the tensor case. This is
the user-facing entry point that chains together all the spinor registration
steps.

## Function

```julia
# In src/spinors/setup.jl (~60 LOC)

define_spinor_structure!(reg::TensorRegistry; manifold::Symbol=:M4, metric::Symbol=:g)
```

Calls in order:
1. define_spinor_bundles!(reg; manifold)       -- :SL2C, :SL2C_dot VBundles
2. define_spin_metric!(reg; manifold)          -- epsilon_{AB}, epsilon_{A'B'}, deltas
3. define_soldering_form!(reg; manifold, metric) -- sigma^a_{AA'}
4. Registers fundamental identities as rewrite rules

```julia
# Macro form:
@spinor_manifold M4 metric=g
# Expands to: define_spinor_structure!(current_registry(); manifold=:M4, metric=:g)
```

## Rewrite rules registered

1. sigma^a_{AA'} sigma_{a}^{BB'} -> delta^B_A delta^{B'}_{A'}
2. sigma^a_{AA'} sigma^{b AA'} -> g^{ab}
3. epsilon_{AB} epsilon^{AB} -> 2
4. epsilon_{A'B'} epsilon^{A'B'} -> 2

## Prerequisites

Requires manifold and metric already registered (via @manifold or define_metric!).
Errors if :SL2C already registered on a different manifold.

## Ground truth

- Penrose & Rindler Vol 1 (1984), Ch 3: complete spinor setup
- xAct: DefSpinStructure[M, sigma] one-liner

## Tests

1. @spinor_manifold M4 metric=g succeeds after @manifold M4 dim=4 metric=g
2. After setup: has_vbundle(reg, :SL2C) == true
3. After setup: has_tensor(reg, :eps_spin) == true
4. After setup: has_tensor(reg, :sigma) == true
5. sigma identity: simplify(sigma^a_{AA'} sigma_{a}^{BB'}) = delta^B_A delta^{B'}_{A'}
6. Error if called before @manifold
title: Spinors: Implement @spinor_manifold macro and define_spinor_structure! one-liner

description: Implement algebraic classification of spacetimes in TensorGR.jl. Petrov classification of Weyl tensor (types I, II, D, III, N, O) via invariants I, J. Segre classification of Ricci tensor. Energy condition checker (null, weak, strong, dominant). Validate with Schwarzschild (Type D), FRW (Type O), pp-wave (Type N). Ground truth: Stephani et al, 'Exact Solutions of Einstein's Field Equations' (2003) Ch 4-5; Penrose & Rindler Vol 1, Sec 8.7. New directory: src/classification/.
title: Exact Solution Identification (xIdeal)

description:
## Summary

Design the algebraic classification system for spacetimes in TensorGR.jl. This research issue produced the detailed implementation plan for the Petrov classification pipeline, Segre classification, and energy condition checking.

## Design Decisions

### 1. Algorithm Choice: Hybrid Invariant + Scalar Approach

The Petrov classification uses a two-pronged approach:
- **Primary**: Compute Petrov invariants I, J directly from the Weyl tensor components (frame-independent, no tetrad needed).
- **Disambiguation**: When I^3 = 27J^2 (algebraically special), use Weyl scalars Psi_0...Psi_4 and the Q-matrix rank to distinguish Type II from Type D.
- **Fallback**: When no null tetrad is available, use the 6x6 Weyl bivector eigenvalue structure.

### 2. Type Hierarchy

```
@enum PetrovType TypeI TypeII TypeD TypeIII TypeN TypeO

struct SegreType
    notation::String
    eigenvalues::Vector
    multiplicities::Vector{Int}
    jordan_sizes::Vector{Vector{Int}}
    is_degenerate::Bool
end

struct EnergyConditionResult
    NEC::Any
    WEC::Any
    SEC::Any
    DEC::Any
    rho::Any
    pressures::Vector
end
```

### 3. Directory Structure

New directory: `src/classification/` with files:
- `weyl_scalars.jl` -- Psi_0...Psi_4 from Weyl tensor + null tetrad (~80 LOC)
- `petrov_invariants.jl` -- I, J from Weyl tensor components (~100 LOC)
- `petrov_classify.jl` -- PetrovType enum + classification engine (~150 LOC)
- `segre_classify.jl` -- SegreType + Segre classification (~130 LOC)
- `energy_conditions.jl` -- Energy condition checker (~120 LOC)

Total: ~580 LOC new code + ~270 LOC tests.

### 4. Integration with Existing Codebase

- **Weyl tensor**: Uses `metric_weyl(Riem, Ric, R, g, ginv, dim)` from `src/components/metric_compute.jl` which returns C_{abcd} all-down.
- **Ricci tensor**: Uses `metric_ricci(Riem, dim)` from same file.
- **CTensor**: Numeric operations via existing `ctensor_contract`, `ctensor_inverse`.
- **LinearAlgebra**: Uses `eigvals`, `eigvecs`, `rank`, `det`, `inv` from LinearAlgebra (already a dependency).
- **No NP dependency**: The Weyl scalar function takes the tetrad as input vectors, so it does not require the NP formalism infrastructure (TGR-2d4 / TGR-1gv epic). The NP epic provides a *registration* layer on top; this classification module works at the component level.

### 5. Dependency Graph

```
TGR-1t9.1 (this issue: design)
    |
    +---> TGR-1t9.2 (Weyl scalars Psi_0...Psi_4)
    |         |
    |         +---> TGR-1t9.3 (Petrov invariants I, J)
    |         |         |
    |         +---------+--> TGR-1t9.4 (Petrov classification engine)
    |                            |
    +---> TGR-1t9.5 (Segre)     |
    |         |                  |
    +---> TGR-1t9.6 (Energy)    |
              |                  |
              +---------+--------+--> TGR-1t9.7 (Schwarzschild = Type D)
                                          |
                                          +--> TGR-1t9.8 (FRW = Type O)
                                                    |
                                                    +--> TGR-1t9.9 (pp-wave = Type N)
```

Key: TGR-1t9.5 (Segre) and TGR-1t9.6 (Energy conditions) are independent of the Petrov chain. They can be developed in parallel after this design issue. All three converge at TGR-1t9.7 (Schwarzschild validation).

### 6. Ground Truth References

- Stephani, Kramer, MacCallum, Hoenselaers, Herlt: "Exact Solutions of Einstein's Field Equations" (2003), Ch 4-5
- Penrose & Rindler: "Spinors and Space-Time" Vol 1 (1984), Sec 8.7
- Newman & Penrose: J. Math. Phys. 3, 566 (1962) -- NP formalism
- Kinnersley: J. Math. Phys. 10, 1195 (1969) -- Kinnersley tetrad
- Hawking & Ellis: "The Large Scale Structure of Space-Time" (1973), Ch 4 -- energy conditions

## Acceptance Criteria (completed)

Each child issue (TGR-1t9.2 through TGR-1t9.9) now has:
- Self-contained description with algorithm details and formulas
- Explicit function signatures with docstrings
- LOC estimates
- STRING MATCH test criteria
- Ground truth references with equation numbers
- Integration notes referencing specific existing codebase functions
title: Research: Design Petrov classification algorithm for TensorGR.jl

description:
## Summary

Implement the Weyl scalar computation Ψ₀...Ψ₄ from Weyl tensor components and a null tetrad. This is the foundational layer of the Petrov classification pipeline: given the all-down Weyl tensor C_{abcd} (computed by `metric_weyl` in `src/components/metric_compute.jl`) and a null tetrad (l, n, m, mbar), project to obtain the five complex Newman-Penrose Weyl scalars.

## Depends On

- Weyl tensor component computation already exists: `metric_weyl(Riem, Ric, R, g, ginv, dim)` in `src/components/metric_compute.jl` returns C_{abcd} as a rank-4 Array.
- Null tetrad construction is handled by the NP formalism epic (TGR-1gv, TGR-z5d). However, THIS issue is self-contained: the function takes a tetrad as input (4 vectors of length dim), so it does not require the NP registration infrastructure. The caller provides the tetrad.

## Definitions (Newman-Penrose, J. Math. Phys. 3, 566, 1962)

The five Weyl scalars are contractions of C_{abcd} with null tetrad vectors:

```
Ψ₀ = C_{abcd} l^a m^b l^c m^d
Ψ₁ = C_{abcd} l^a n^b l^c m^d
Ψ₂ = C_{abcd} l^a m^b m̄^c n^d
Ψ₃ = C_{abcd} l^a n^b m̄^c n^d
Ψ₄ = C_{abcd} n^a m̄^b n^c m̄^d
```

Null tetrad normalization: l_a n^a = -1, m_a m̄^a = +1, all other products zero. Completeness: g^{ab} = -l^a n^b - n^a l^b + m^a m̄^b + m̄^a m^b.

## File Location

`src/classification/weyl_scalars.jl` (new file, new directory `src/classification/`)

## Function Signatures

```julia
"""
    weyl_scalars(Weyl::Array{T,4}, l::Vector, n::Vector, m::Vector, mbar::Vector) where T
        -> NamedTuple{(:Psi0, :Psi1, :Psi2, :Psi3, :Psi4)}

Compute the 5 Newman-Penrose Weyl scalars from the all-down Weyl tensor
C_{abcd} and null tetrad vectors (l^a, n^a, m^a, m̄^a).

The Weyl tensor must be the all-down form C_{abcd} (as returned by `metric_weyl`).
The tetrad vectors are contravariant (upper index). The metric is used implicitly
via the Weyl tensor's index structure.

Returns a NamedTuple with fields Psi0...Psi4.
"""
function weyl_scalars(Weyl::Array{T,4}, l::Vector, n::Vector,
                      m::Vector, mbar::Vector) where T

"""
    validate_null_tetrad(l, n, m, mbar, g; atol=1e-10) -> Bool

Check that the tetrad satisfies: l·n = -1, m·m̄ = +1, l·l = n·n = 0,
l·m = l·m̄ = n·m = n·m̄ = m·m = m̄·m̄ = 0.
Uses metric g_{ab} to lower indices: v_a = g_{ab} v^b.
"""
function validate_null_tetrad(l, n, m, mbar, g::Matrix; atol=1e-10)
```

## Implementation Notes

- The contraction is a 4-nested sum: Ψ_k = Σ_{a,b,c,d} C_{abcd} * v1^a * v2^b * v3^c * v4^d where (v1,v2,v3,v4) differ for each scalar.
- For symbolic CAS (Symbolics.jl), the entries may be `Num` type -- use generic summation, do not assume numeric.
- The Weyl tensor from `metric_weyl` is already all-down (C_{abcd}), so the tetrad vectors must be contravariant (upper index). This matches the convention in `metric_compute.jl`.
- Complex conjugate: `mbar` is provided explicitly (not computed from `m`), to support both numeric and symbolic inputs where `conj` may not be available.

## Estimated LOC

~80 lines (weyl_scalars function + validate_null_tetrad + helper for single contraction)

## Test Criteria

1. **Schwarzschild in Kinnersley tetrad**: For Schwarzschild metric with M=1, at r=3:
   - Ψ₂ = -M/r³ = -1/27 (only nonzero scalar)
   - Ψ₀ = Ψ₁ = Ψ₃ = Ψ₄ = 0
   - STRING MATCH: `@test isapprox(psi.Psi2, -1/27, atol=1e-12)`
   - STRING MATCH: `@test all(isapprox.(abs.([psi.Psi0, psi.Psi1, psi.Psi3, psi.Psi4]), 0, atol=1e-12))`

2. **Minkowski (flat space)**: All Ψ_k = 0.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test all(v -> isapprox(v, 0, atol=1e-14), [psi.Psi0, psi.Psi1, psi.Psi2, psi.Psi3, psi.Psi4])`

3. **Tetrad validation**: Kinnersley tetrad passes `validate_null_tetrad`.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test validate_null_tetrad(l, n, m, mbar, g) == true`

4. **Consistency**: Verify Ψ₀*Ψ₄ - 4Ψ₁*Ψ₃ + 3Ψ₂² equals the Petrov invariant I (cross-check with TGR-1t9.3).

## Ground Truth References

- Newman & Penrose, J. Math. Phys. 3, 566 (1962), Eqs. (4.2a-e)
- Stephani et al, "Exact Solutions of Einstein's Field Equations" (2003), Eq. (3.59)
- Kinnersley, J. Math. Phys. 10, 1195 (1969) -- Kinnersley tetrad for Schwarzschild
title: xIdeal: Implement Petrov type from Weyl scalars Ψ₀...Ψ₄

close_reason: Implemented with Stephani Eq 4.19, Schwarzschild I^3=27J^2 verified
description:
## Summary

Implement the two complex Petrov invariants I and J from the Weyl tensor. These are frame-independent scalar invariants that encode the algebraic type of the Weyl tensor without requiring a choice of null tetrad. They are the primary tool for Petrov classification: the condition I³ = 27J² characterizes algebraically special spacetimes.

## Depends On

- TGR-1t9.2 (Weyl scalars): Needed for the scalar-based formulas (secondary route).
- `metric_weyl` in `src/components/metric_compute.jl`: Provides C_{abcd} components.
- `metric_kretschmann` pattern: Shows how to contract rank-4 tensors with the metric.

## Definitions (Stephani et al, "Exact Solutions", Eqs. 4.19-4.20)

### From Weyl scalars (NP formalism):
```
I = Ψ₀Ψ₄ - 4Ψ₁Ψ₃ + 3Ψ₂²
J = | Ψ₀  Ψ₁  Ψ₂ |
    | Ψ₁  Ψ₂  Ψ₃ | = det(Q)
    | Ψ₂  Ψ₃  Ψ₄ |
```
where Q is the 3x3 symmetric matrix Q_{ij} = Ψ_{i+j} (i,j = 0,1,2).

### From Weyl tensor components (frame-independent):
```
I = (1/8)(C_{abcd}C^{abcd} + i C_{abcd} *C^{abcd})
  = (1/8)(C_{abcd}C^{abcd} + i/2 ε^{abef} C_{efcd} C^{abcd})  -- NOT IMPLEMENTED (requires ε)
```

More practically, define the "Weyl bivector" matrix Q_{AB} where A,B are bivector indices:
```
C^a_{bcd} = Q^{AB} (e_A)^a_b (e_B)_{cd}    -- bivector decomposition
I = Q^{AB} Q_{BA}  (trace of Q²)
J = Q^{AB} Q_{BC} Q^{C}_A  (trace of Q³)
```

### From components directly (the approach to implement):

Given C_{abcd} all-down and g^{ab} inverse metric:
```
# Raise indices to get C^{ab}_{cd}
C_up_ab_dn_cd[a,b,c,d] = Σ_{e,f} g^{ae} g^{bf} C_{efcd}

# I = (1/2) C^{ab}_{cd} C^{cd}_{ab}   (self-contraction of the Weyl endomorphism)
I = (1/2) Σ_{a,b,c,d} C_up[a,b,c,d] * C_up[c,d,a,b]

# J = (1/6) C^{ab}_{cd} C^{cd}_{ef} C^{ef}_{ab}   (triple trace)
J = (1/6) Σ_{a,b,c,d,e,f} C_up[a,b,c,d] * C_up[c,d,e,f] * C_up[e,f,a,b]
```

IMPORTANT: I and J are in general COMPLEX. For Lorentzian 4D metrics, the real part of I gives (1/8)C_{abcd}C^{abcd} and the imaginary part gives the dual contraction. For real metrics at numeric evaluation, I and J will be real.

## File Location

`src/classification/petrov_invariants.jl` (new file in `src/classification/`)

## Function Signatures

```julia
"""
    petrov_invariants(Weyl::Array{T,4}, ginv::Matrix; dim::Int=4) where T
        -> NamedTuple{(:I, :J)}

Compute the Petrov invariants I and J from the all-down Weyl tensor C_{abcd}
and inverse metric g^{ab}.

I = (1/2) C^{ab}_{cd} C^{cd}_{ab}
J = (1/6) C^{ab}_{cd} C^{cd}_{ef} C^{ef}_{ab}

Returns a NamedTuple with fields I, J.
"""
function petrov_invariants(Weyl::Array{T,4}, ginv::Matrix; dim::Int=4) where T

"""
    petrov_invariants_from_scalars(Psi0, Psi1, Psi2, Psi3, Psi4)
        -> NamedTuple{(:I, :J)}

Compute Petrov invariants from NP Weyl scalars (shortcut when tetrad is available).
I = Ψ₀Ψ₄ - 4Ψ₁Ψ₃ + 3Ψ₂²
J = det([Ψ₀ Ψ₁ Ψ₂; Ψ₁ Ψ₂ Ψ₃; Ψ₂ Ψ₃ Ψ₄])
"""
function petrov_invariants_from_scalars(Psi0, Psi1, Psi2, Psi3, Psi4)

"""
    _raise_weyl_pair(Weyl::Array{T,4}, ginv::Matrix, dim::Int) where T
        -> Array{T,4}

Compute C^{ab}_{cd} = g^{ae} g^{bf} C_{efcd} (raise first two indices).
Internal helper used by petrov_invariants.
"""
```

## Implementation Notes

- The `_raise_weyl_pair` helper is the key computational kernel: a 6-nested loop (a,b,c,d over dim, plus two sums over e,f). For dim=4 this is 4^6 = 4096 iterations -- fast enough.
- For symbolic inputs (Symbolics.jl `Num` type), the summation must use generic `+` and `*`. Initialize accumulators with `zero(T)` where T is the element type.
- The J invariant involves a triple contraction (8-nested loop). Factor it as two matrix multiplications of the "Weyl endomorphism" to reduce to O(dim^6) instead of O(dim^8):
  ```
  Q[a,b,c,d] = C^{ab}_{cd}
  QQ[a,b,e,f] = Σ_{c,d} Q[a,b,c,d] * Q[c,d,e,f]    # Q²
  J = (1/6) Σ_{a,b,e,f} QQ[a,b,e,f] * Q[e,f,a,b]    # Tr(Q³)
  ```
- Factor 1/2 for I and 1/6 for J come from the normalization convention matching Stephani. VERIFY against Schwarzschild ground truth.

## Estimated LOC

~100 lines (_raise_weyl_pair + petrov_invariants + petrov_invariants_from_scalars)

## Test Criteria

1. **Schwarzschild (Type D)**: For Schwarzschild with M=1, at r=3:
   - I = 12M²/r⁶ = 12/729 = 4/243 (real, from Kretschner/48)
   - J = -12M³/r⁹ (with appropriate normalization)
   - KEY TEST: I³ = 27J² (exactly, to floating-point precision)
   - STRING MATCH: `@test isapprox(inv.I^3, 27 * inv.J^2, rtol=1e-10)`

2. **Minkowski (Type O)**: I = 0, J = 0.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test isapprox(inv.I, 0, atol=1e-14) && isapprox(inv.J, 0, atol=1e-14)`

3. **Cross-check with NP scalars**: For Schwarzschild, verify that `petrov_invariants` and `petrov_invariants_from_scalars` agree:
   - STRING MATCH: `@test isapprox(inv_tensor.I, inv_scalars.I, rtol=1e-10)`

4. **Normalization consistency**: Verify I = (1/16) * Kretschmann for vacuum (where Weyl = Riemann):
   - For Schwarzschild: Kretschmer = 48M²/r⁶, so I should equal 48M²/r⁶ / 8 = 6M²/r⁶.
   - IMPORTANT: The exact normalization factor depends on convention. Pin it with the Schwarzschild test.

- Penrose & Rindler, "Spinors and Space-time" Vol 1 (1984), Sec 8.7

description:
## Summary

Implement the Petrov classification decision algorithm that determines the algebraic type (I, II, III, D, N, O) from the invariants I, J and supplementary data. This is the core classification engine: given I and J (from TGR-1t9.3), plus optionally the Weyl scalars (from TGR-1t9.2) or Weyl tensor components, return the Petrov type.

## Depends On

- TGR-1t9.3 (Petrov invariants I, J)
- TGR-1t9.2 (Weyl scalars, for II/D disambiguation)

## Algorithm (Stephani et al, Table 4.3; Penrose & Rindler Vol 1, Sec 8.7)

### Decision Tree

```
1. Is C_{abcd} = 0 ?
   YES -> Type O (conformally flat)
   NO  -> continue

2. Is I = 0 AND J = 0 ?
   YES -> algebraically special with vanishing invariants:
          -> Compute auxiliary: D = Ψ₀Ψ₄ - Ψ₁Ψ₃ (or from tensor: C^{ab}_{cd} C^{cd}_{ab} parts)
          -> If ALL Ψ_k = 0 except one: Type N
          -> If Ψ₂ ≠ 0 or exactly two non-adjacent Ψ_k vanish: Type III
          (In practice: check Ψ₂. If Ψ₂=0, check Ψ₃. If Ψ₃≠0, Type III. If Ψ₃=0, Type N.)
   NO  -> continue

3. Compute Δ = I³ - 27J²

4. Is Δ = 0 (i.e., I³ = 27J²) ?
   YES -> algebraically special with nonzero invariants:
          Need to distinguish Type II from Type D.
          -> Type D: Weyl tensor has exactly 2 double principal null directions.
             Equivalently, in a canonical frame: Ψ₀ = Ψ₁ = Ψ₃ = Ψ₄ = 0, Ψ₂ ≠ 0.
          -> Type II: Weyl tensor has 1 double + 2 simple principal null directions.
          DISAMBIGUATION METHOD:
          Compute the "Weyl polynomial" P(x) = Ψ₀ + 4Ψ₁x + 6Ψ₂x² + 4Ψ₃x³ + Ψ₄x⁴.
          Multiplicity of roots determines type:
            - One double root + two simple roots -> Type II
            - Two double roots -> Type D
          ALTERNATIVE (numeric, no root-finding):
          Compute L_{abcd} = C_{abcd} - (J/I) g_{a[c}g_{d]b} (the "alignment" tensor).
          If L^{ab}_{cd} L^{cd}_{ab} = 0 -> Type D.
          PRACTICAL APPROACH (for numeric Weyl scalars):
          Use the 3x3 Q-matrix: Q = [Ψ₀ Ψ₁ Ψ₂; Ψ₁ Ψ₂ Ψ₃; Ψ₂ Ψ₃ Ψ₄].
          Compute eigenvalues of Q. Type D has one eigenvalue with multiplicity 3 (i.e., Q = λ·I₃).
          Actually: rank(Q - λI) for the repeated eigenvalue. Type D: rank ≤ 1. Type II: rank = 2.
   NO  -> Type I (algebraically general, 4 distinct PNDs)
```

### For purely numeric inputs (without Weyl scalars):

When only I and J are available (no tetrad), the II/D distinction requires additional computation. Implement a fallback using the "N-test" invariant:

```
N = I² - 3J·(trace of Weyl⁴)/(trace of Weyl²)²
```

But in practice, this is complex. The recommended approach:
1. Primary path: Use Weyl scalars if available (tetrad-dependent but unambiguous).
2. Fallback path: Compute eigenvalues of the 6x6 Weyl bivector matrix.

## File Location

`src/classification/petrov_classify.jl` (new file in `src/classification/`)

## Function Signatures

```julia
"""
    PetrovType

Enum for the six Petrov types.
"""
@enum PetrovType TypeI TypeII TypeD TypeIII TypeN TypeO

"""
    petrov_classify(Weyl::Array{T,4}, ginv::Matrix; dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10) where T
        -> PetrovType

Classify the Petrov type from the Weyl tensor components.
Uses the invariant decision tree plus eigenvalue analysis for disambiguation.
"""
function petrov_classify(Weyl::Array{T,4}, ginv::Matrix; dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10) where T

"""
    petrov_classify(I, J; Psi0=nothing, Psi1=nothing, Psi2=nothing,
                    Psi3=nothing, Psi4=nothing, atol=1e-10) -> PetrovType

Classify from pre-computed invariants and optional Weyl scalars.
If Weyl scalars are provided, uses them for II/D and III/N disambiguation.
"""
function petrov_classify(I, J; Psi0=nothing, Psi1=nothing, Psi2=nothing,
                          Psi3=nothing, Psi4=nothing, atol=1e-10)

"""
    _weyl_is_zero(Weyl::Array, atol) -> Bool

Check if the Weyl tensor vanishes (all components below atol).
"""

"""
    _disambiguate_II_D(Psi0, Psi1, Psi2, Psi3, Psi4; atol=1e-10) -> PetrovType

Distinguish Type II from Type D using the Q-matrix rank.
Q = [Ψ₀ Ψ₁ Ψ₂; Ψ₁ Ψ₂ Ψ₃; Ψ₂ Ψ₃ Ψ₄]
Type D: rank(Q) = 1 (all 2x2 minors vanish)
Type II: rank(Q) = 2
"""

"""
    _disambiguate_III_N(Psi0, Psi1, Psi2, Psi3, Psi4; atol=1e-10) -> PetrovType

Distinguish Type III from Type N using the Weyl scalar pattern.
Type N: exactly one Ψ_k ≠ 0 (after frame rotation to canonical form).
In practice: check rank of Q-matrix: rank 1 = Type N, rank 2 = Type III.
Actually simpler: If Ψ₂ = 0 and (Ψ₁ = 0 or Ψ₃ = 0) it is more degenerate.
Use: K = Ψ₁Ψ₃ - Ψ₂². K = 0 for Types III and N.
Then: Ψ₁² + Ψ₃² = 0 -> Type N. Otherwise Type III.
Actually in canonical frame for I=J=0: Ψ₀=Ψ₁=0. Then: Ψ₂=0 -> check Ψ₃.
Ψ₃=0 -> Type N. Ψ₃≠0 -> Type III.
"""
```

## Implementation Notes

- The `atol` parameter is crucial for numeric classification. Symbolic inputs should use exact arithmetic (no tolerance needed).
- For the Weyl tensor approach (no tetrad): construct the 6x6 "bivector" representation of the Weyl tensor Q_{AB} where A,B are antisymmetric pairs [ab]. The eigenvalues of Q determine the type:
  - 3 distinct eigenvalues -> Type I
  - 1 double + 1 simple eigenvalue -> Type II
  - 2 double eigenvalues... etc.
  This is the FALLBACK when Weyl scalars are not available.
- Use `LinearAlgebra.eigvals` for numeric eigenvalue computation.
- For symbolic inputs: classification may return a conditional expression or require the user to evaluate at specific points.

## Estimated LOC

~150 lines (PetrovType enum + petrov_classify main + disambiguation helpers + bivector fallback)

## Test Criteria

1. **Schwarzschild -> Type D**:
   - STRING MATCH: `@test petrov_classify(Weyl_schw, ginv_schw) == TypeD`

2. **Minkowski -> Type O**:
   - STRING MATCH: `@test petrov_classify(zeros(4,4,4,4), ginv_mink) == TypeO`

3. **pp-wave -> Type N**:
   - STRING MATCH: `@test petrov_classify(Weyl_pp, ginv_pp) == TypeN`

4. **FRW -> Type O**:
   - STRING MATCH: `@test petrov_classify(Weyl_frw, ginv_frw) == TypeO`

5. **From invariants + scalars (Schwarzschild)**:
   - STRING MATCH: `@test petrov_classify(I_schw, J_schw; Psi0=0, Psi1=0, Psi2=psi2, Psi3=0, Psi4=0) == TypeD`

6. **Generic Type I metric**: Construct a metric with all 5 Weyl scalars nonzero (e.g., a general Bianchi model or Kasner metric with generic exponents).
   - STRING MATCH: `@test petrov_classify(Weyl_generic, ginv_generic) == TypeI`

## Ground Truth References

- Stephani et al, "Exact Solutions" (2003), Table 4.3
- Penrose & Rindler, "Spinors and Space-time" Vol 1 (1984), Sec 8.7
- d'Inverno, "Introducing Einstein's Relativity" (1992), Ch 6.5
- Coley, Hervik, Pelavas, Class. Quant. Grav. 26, 025013 (2009) -- algorithmic classification

description:
## Summary

Implement Segre classification of the Ricci tensor (or equivalently the trace-free Ricci tensor S_{ab} = R_{ab} - (1/4)R g_{ab} in 4D). The Segre type describes the eigenvalue structure of the mixed Ricci tensor R^a_b, providing a complementary algebraic characterization to the Petrov type. Together, Petrov + Segre fully classify the local geometry.

## Depends On

- `metric_ricci` in `src/components/metric_compute.jl` for R_{bd} components.
- `metric_ricci_scalar` for R.
- CTensor infrastructure: `ctensor_inverse` for g^{ab}.
- LinearAlgebra: `eigvals` for eigenvalue computation.
- Does NOT depend on TGR-1t9.2/3/4 (Petrov classification is independent).

## Definitions (Stephani et al, "Exact Solutions", Ch 5)

The Segre classification is based on the Jordan normal form of the mixed tensor R^a_b = g^{ac} R_{cb}. In 4D Lorentzian signature:

### Eigenvalue structure:
- Each eigenvalue can be real or come in complex conjugate pairs.
- The Jordan block structure determines the "Segre type".
- Notation: digits represent block sizes, commas separate timelike from spacelike sectors.
  - `{1,111}` = 4 distinct real eigenvalues (generic)
  - `{(1,1)(11)}` = 2 pairs of equal eigenvalues (e.g., Schwarzschild vacuum)
  - `{(1,111)}` = one eigenvalue with multiplicity 4, 1+3 degeneracy (perfect fluid, FRW)
  - `{2,11}` = one 2x2 Jordan block (null electromagnetic field)
  - `{(1,1)11}` = timelike-spacelike pair equal, two others distinct
  - Round brackets `()` denote equal eigenvalues.

### Algorithm:
1. Compute R^a_b = g^{ac} R_{cb} (raise first index).
2. Compute eigenvalues λ_1, ..., λ_4.
3. Group by multiplicity and determine Jordan block structure.
4. Assign Segre type based on the pattern.

### Refinement -- the trace-free Ricci (Plebanski tensor):
For vacuum (R_{ab} = 0), all eigenvalues are zero and Segre type is {(1,111)} trivially. The non-trivial classification applies to the energy-momentum tensor T_{ab} via the Einstein equations.

## File Location

`src/classification/segre_classify.jl` (new file in `src/classification/`)

## Function Signatures

```julia
"""
    SegreType

Struct holding the Segre classification result.
"""
struct SegreType
    notation::String           # e.g., "{(1,1)(11)}"
    eigenvalues::Vector        # eigenvalues of R^a_b (may be complex)
    multiplicities::Vector{Int} # multiplicity of each distinct eigenvalue
    jordan_sizes::Vector{Vector{Int}}  # Jordan block sizes for each eigenvalue
    is_degenerate::Bool        # true if non-trivial Jordan blocks exist
end

"""
    segre_classify(Ric::Matrix, ginv::Matrix; dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10) -> SegreType

Classify the Segre type of the Ricci tensor from its components R_{ab}
and the inverse metric g^{ab}. Computes R^a_b = g^{ac} R_{cb} and
analyzes its eigenvalue/Jordan structure.
"""
function segre_classify(Ric::Matrix, ginv::Matrix; dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10)

"""
    segre_classify(R_mixed::Matrix; dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10) -> SegreType

Classify from the pre-computed mixed Ricci tensor R^a_b.
"""
function segre_classify(R_mixed::Matrix; dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10)

"""
    _segre_notation(eigenvalues, multiplicities, jordan_sizes, dim; sig=(-,+,+,+))
        -> String

Format the Segre notation string from eigenvalue data.
Groups timelike and spacelike sectors based on metric signature.
"""

"""
    _jordan_structure(M::Matrix; atol=1e-10) -> (eigenvalues, multiplicities, jordan_sizes)

Compute eigenvalues and Jordan block structure of a matrix.
Uses eigvals + rank analysis of (M - λI)^k for each eigenvalue.
"""
```

## Implementation Notes

- The core computation is straightforward: R^a_b = Σ_c g^{ac} R_{cb}, then eigvals(R_mixed).
- Jordan block detection: For each eigenvalue λ of multiplicity m, compute rank(R^a_b - λ δ^a_b). If rank deficit > multiplicity, there are non-trivial Jordan blocks. Specifically, the number of Jordan blocks of size ≥ k is dim - rank((R - λI)^k).
- For Lorentzian signature, eigenvalues can be complex. Use `eigvals` from LinearAlgebra which handles this.
- Timelike vs spacelike sector assignment: In (-,+,+,+) signature, the timelike eigenvector has g_{ab}v^a v^b < 0. This requires computing eigenvectors, not just eigenvalues.
- For vacuum spacetimes (R_{ab} = 0), return the trivial Segre type `{(1,111)}` immediately (all eigenvalues zero).
- For symbolic metrics: eigenvalue computation requires a CAS. Return symbolic eigenvalues and defer classification to numeric evaluation. Add a method that takes pre-computed eigenvalues.

## Estimated LOC

~130 lines (SegreType struct + segre_classify + jordan_structure + notation formatting)

## Test Criteria

1. **Schwarzschild (vacuum)**: R_{ab} = 0, Segre type `{(1,111)}` (all zero eigenvalues).
   - STRING MATCH: `@test st.notation == "{(1,111)}"`

2. **FRW (perfect fluid)**: For FRW with dust (p=0): T_{ab} = ρ u_a u_b, Segre type `{(1,111)}` (degenerate).
   For FRW with general perfect fluid (ρ ≠ 0, p ≠ 0): T_{ab} = (ρ+p)u_a u_b + p g_{ab}, Segre type `{1,(111)}` (one timelike eigenvalue ρ, three spatial eigenvalues p).
   - STRING MATCH: `@test st.notation == "{1,(111)}"` (for generic perfect fluid)

3. **de Sitter**: R_{ab} = Λ g_{ab}, all eigenvalues equal to Λ. Segre type `{(1,111)}`.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test st.notation == "{(1,111)}"`

4. **Eigenvalue count**: For a diagonal metric with 4 distinct Ricci eigenvalues, Segre type should be `{1,111}`.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test st.notation == "{1,111}"`

## Ground Truth References

- Stephani et al, "Exact Solutions" (2003), Ch 5, Tables 5.1-5.2
- Plebanski, Acta Phys. Polon. 26, 963 (1964)
- Hall, "Symmetries and Curvature Structure in GR" (2004), Ch 7

description:
## Summary

Implement energy condition verification from the Ricci/Einstein tensor components. The four standard pointwise energy conditions (NEC, WEC, SEC, DEC) are algebraic inequalities on the energy-momentum tensor T_{ab} (or equivalently R_{ab} via the Einstein equations). This module evaluates these conditions at given spacetime points for numeric metrics, or returns symbolic inequality expressions for symbolic metrics.

## Depends On

- `metric_ricci`, `metric_ricci_scalar`, `metric_einstein` in `src/components/metric_compute.jl`.
- CTensor infrastructure for index raising/lowering.
- Does NOT depend on Petrov or Segre classification (TGR-1t9.2/3/4/5). This is an independent classification axis.

## Definitions (Hawking & Ellis, "Large Scale Structure", Ch 4; Wald, "General Relativity", Ch 9)

Given energy-momentum tensor T_{ab} (obtained from G_{ab} = 8πG T_{ab}):

### Null Energy Condition (NEC):
T_{ab} k^a k^b >= 0 for all null vectors k^a (k_a k^a = 0).
Equivalently: R_{ab} k^a k^b >= 0 for null k^a.
In terms of eigenvalues of T^a_b: ρ + p_i >= 0 for all principal pressures p_i.

### Weak Energy Condition (WEC):
T_{ab} t^a t^b >= 0 for all timelike vectors t^a (t_a t^a < 0).
Equivalently: ρ >= 0 and ρ + p_i >= 0.
Implies NEC.

### Strong Energy Condition (SEC):
(T_{ab} - (1/2)T g_{ab}) t^a t^b >= 0 for all timelike t^a.
Equivalently: R_{ab} t^a t^b >= 0 for all timelike t^a.
In terms of eigenvalues: ρ + Σ p_i >= 0 and ρ + p_i >= 0.

### Dominant Energy Condition (DEC):
For any future-pointing timelike t^a, -T^a_b t^b is future-pointing causal (timelike or null).
Equivalently: ρ >= |p_i| for all principal pressures.
Implies WEC (and hence NEC).

### For a perfect fluid T_{ab} = (ρ+p) u_a u_b + p g_{ab}:
- NEC: ρ + p >= 0
- WEC: ρ >= 0 and ρ + p >= 0
- SEC: ρ + 3p >= 0 and ρ + p >= 0
- DEC: ρ >= |p|

### Practical evaluation for a general metric:
Diagonalize T^a_b to find eigenvalues. The timelike eigenvalue is -ρ, the spacelike eigenvalues are p_1, p_2, p_3. Then check the algebraic conditions above.

## File Location

`src/classification/energy_conditions.jl` (new file in `src/classification/`)

## Function Signatures

```julia
"""
    EnergyConditionResult

Result of energy condition evaluation.
"""
struct EnergyConditionResult
    NEC::Any   # Bool for numeric, symbolic expression for symbolic
    WEC::Any
    SEC::Any
    DEC::Any
    rho::Any       # energy density (timelike eigenvalue)
    pressures::Vector  # principal pressures (spacelike eigenvalues)
end

"""
    check_energy_conditions(Ric::Matrix, R, g::Matrix, ginv::Matrix;
                            dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10) -> EnergyConditionResult

Check all four energy conditions from the Ricci tensor, Ricci scalar, and metric.
Computes T_{ab} from the Einstein equations (assuming 8πG=1).
"""
function check_energy_conditions(Ric::Matrix, R, g::Matrix, ginv::Matrix;
                                  dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10)

"""
    check_energy_conditions(T_mixed::Matrix, g::Matrix;
                            dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10) -> EnergyConditionResult

Check from the pre-computed mixed energy-momentum tensor T^a_b.
"""
function check_energy_conditions(T_mixed::Matrix, g::Matrix;
                                  dim::Int=4, atol=1e-10)

"""
    _eigendecompose_stress_energy(T_mixed::Matrix, g::Matrix; atol=1e-10)
        -> (rho, pressures)

Diagonalize T^a_b and identify the timelike eigenvalue (energy density)
and spacelike eigenvalues (principal pressures).
The timelike eigenvector satisfies g_{ab} v^a v^b < 0.
"""
```

## Implementation Notes

- The key step is diagonalizing T^a_b. For Lorentzian signature, the eigenvalues of T^a_b may be complex if T_{ab} is not a "physical" energy-momentum tensor. Handle gracefully.
- Identifying timelike vs spacelike eigenvectors: compute g_{ab} v^a v^b for each eigenvector v. Negative = timelike, positive = spacelike. This requires `eigvecs` from LinearAlgebra.
- For vacuum (T_{ab} = 0): all conditions trivially satisfied, return immediately.
- For symbolic metrics: cannot check inequalities. Return symbolic expressions for the eigenvalues and let the user evaluate.
- The conversion from Ricci to T_{ab}: T_{ab} = (1/8πG)(R_{ab} - (1/2)R g_{ab}). With 8πG=1: T_{ab} = G_{ab} = R_{ab} - (1/2)R g_{ab}. Use the existing `metric_einstein` function.

## Estimated LOC

~120 lines (EnergyConditionResult + check_energy_conditions + eigendecompose helper + condition checkers)

## Test Criteria

1. **Schwarzschild (vacuum)**: T_{ab} = 0, all conditions trivially satisfied.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test ec.NEC == true && ec.WEC == true && ec.SEC == true && ec.DEC == true`

2. **de Sitter (Λ > 0)**: T_{ab} = -(Λ/8πG) g_{ab}. This is ρ = Λ/8πG, p = -Λ/8πG (dark energy).
   - NEC: ρ + p = 0, so NEC satisfied (marginal).
   - SEC violated: ρ + 3p = -2Λ/8πG < 0.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test ec.NEC == true && ec.SEC == false`

3. **Dust FRW**: ρ > 0, p = 0. All conditions satisfied.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test ec.NEC == true && ec.WEC == true && ec.SEC == true && ec.DEC == true`

4. **Minkowski**: T = 0, all satisfied.
   - STRING MATCH: `@test ec.NEC == true`

## Ground Truth References

- Hawking & Ellis, "The Large Scale Structure of Space-Time" (1973), Ch 4.3
- Wald, "General Relativity" (1984), Ch 9.2
- Visser, "Lorentzian Wormholes" (1996), Ch 12 (energy condition catalog)
- Curiel, arXiv:1405.0403 (2017) -- modern review of energy conditions

description:
## Summary

End-to-end validation of the Petrov + Segre classification pipeline for the Schwarzschild metric. This is the primary integration test: build the Schwarzschild metric from components, compute all curvature tensors, construct a null tetrad (Kinnersley), compute Weyl scalars and Petrov invariants, and verify Petrov Type D and Segre type.

## Depends On

- TGR-1t9.2 (Weyl scalars)
- TGR-1t9.3 (Petrov invariants)
- TGR-1t9.4 (Petrov classification)
- TGR-1t9.5 (Segre classification)
- TGR-1t9.6 (Energy conditions)

## Test Setup

### Schwarzschild metric (Schwarzschild coordinates):
```
ds² = -(1 - 2M/r) dt² + (1 - 2M/r)⁻¹ dr² + r² dθ² + r² sin²θ dφ²

g_{ab} = diag(-(1-2M/r), (1-2M/r)⁻¹, r², r²sin²θ)
```

Use M = 1, evaluate at r = 3, θ = π/2 (outside horizon, equatorial plane).

### Kinnersley null tetrad (Kinnersley, J. Math. Phys. 10, 1195, 1969):
For Schwarzschild in standard coordinates (t, r, θ, φ):
```
l^a = ((r²/(r-2M)), 1, 0, 0) / (r-2M)   -- scaled to l·n = -1
     Actually: l^a = (1/(1-2M/r), 1, 0, 0) (outgoing null, unnormalized)
n^a = (1/2)(1, -(1-2M/r), 0, 0)           (ingoing null)
m^a = (1/(r√2))(0, 0, 1, i/sinθ)          (complex null)
m̄^a = (1/(r√2))(0, 0, 1, -i/sinθ)        (complex conjugate)
```

Normalization check: l_a n^a = g_{ab} l^a n^b = -1. All other inner products = 0.

### Expected results:
1. **Weyl scalars**:
   - Ψ₀ = Ψ₁ = Ψ₃ = Ψ₄ = 0
   - Ψ₂ = -M/r³ = -1/27 (at r=3, M=1)

2. **Petrov invariants**:
   - I = 3Ψ₂² = 3/729 = 1/243
   - J = Ψ₂³ = -1/19683
   - Check: I³ = 27J² (algebraically special)
   - Verify: 27 * (1/19683)² = 27/387420489 = 1/14348907 = (1/243)³. YES.

3. **Petrov type**: Type D

4. **Segre type**: `{(1,111)}` (vacuum, all Ricci eigenvalues zero)

5. **Energy conditions**: All satisfied (vacuum)

6. **Cross-checks**:
   - Kretschmann scalar K = 48M²/r⁶ = 48/729 (from `metric_kretschmann`)
   - Relation: I should be related to K (in vacuum, Weyl = Riemann, so K = 8*Re(I) for the right normalization).

## File Location

`test/test_petrov_schwarzschild.jl` (new test file)

## Test Code Structure

```julia
@testset "Petrov classification: Schwarzschild" begin
    M = 1; r = 3; theta = pi/2

    # Build metric components
    f = 1 - 2M/r
    g = diagm([-f, 1/f, r^2, r^2 * sin(theta)^2])
    ginv = inv(g)

    # Compute curvature
    # (Use metric_christoffel, metric_riemann, etc. with finite differences
    #  or pre-computed analytic components)

    # Kinnersley tetrad
    l = [1/f, 1.0, 0.0, 0.0]
    n = [0.5, -f/2, 0.0, 0.0]
    m = [0.0, 0.0, 1.0, im/sin(theta)] / (r * sqrt(2))
    mbar = conj.(m)

    # Validate tetrad
    @test validate_null_tetrad(l, n, m, mbar, g) == true

    # Weyl scalars
    psi = weyl_scalars(Weyl, l, n, m, mbar)
    @test isapprox(psi.Psi2, -M/r^3, atol=1e-10)
    @test all(x -> isapprox(abs(x), 0, atol=1e-10), [psi.Psi0, psi.Psi1, psi.Psi3, psi.Psi4])

    # Petrov invariants
    inv = petrov_invariants_from_scalars(psi...)
    @test isapprox(inv.I^3, 27 * inv.J^2, rtol=1e-10)

    # Petrov type
    @test petrov_classify(Weyl, ginv) == TypeD

    # Segre type
    st = segre_classify(Ric, ginv)
    @test st.notation == "{(1,111)}"

    # Energy conditions
    ec = check_energy_conditions(Ric, R, g, ginv)
    @test ec.NEC == true && ec.WEC == true && ec.SEC == true && ec.DEC == true
end
```

## Implementation Notes

- The Schwarzschild Weyl tensor can be computed analytically for exact ground truth:
  C_{trtr} = -2M/r³, C_{tθtθ} = M(r-2M)/r², C_{tφtφ} = M(r-2M)sin²θ/r², etc.
  But using the numeric pipeline (metric_christoffel -> metric_riemann -> metric_weyl) is the real test.
- For the Christoffel/Riemann computation, since we evaluate at a single point, use finite differences for derivatives: deriv_fn(f, x) ≈ (f(x+h) - f(x-h))/(2h). This avoids needing Symbolics.jl for this validation test.
- ALTERNATIVE: Use the Symbolics.jl extension (symbolic_curvature_from_metric) for exact symbolic computation, then substitute r=3.
- The Kinnersley tetrad normalization is tricky. Verify l·n = -1 BEFORE computing Weyl scalars.

## Estimated LOC

~100 lines (test setup + 6 test blocks)

## Test Criteria (STRING MATCH)

1. `@test validate_null_tetrad(l, n, m, mbar, g) == true`
2. `@test isapprox(psi.Psi2, -1/27, atol=1e-10)`
3. `@test isapprox(inv.I^3, 27 * inv.J^2, rtol=1e-10)`
4. `@test petrov_classify(Weyl_schw, ginv_schw) == TypeD`
5. `@test st.notation == "{(1,111)}"`
6. `@test ec.NEC == true`

## Ground Truth References

- Kinnersley, J. Math. Phys. 10, 1195 (1969), Eqs. (3)-(4)
- Stephani et al, "Exact Solutions" (2003), Table 15.1 (Schwarzschild classification)
- d'Inverno, "Introducing Einstein's Relativity" (1992), Example 6.5.2
